elements.sent-2411-001: Speculative Decoding has gained popularity as an effective technique for accelerating the auto-regressive inference process of Large Language Models (LLMs).
elements.sent-2411-002: However, Speculative Decoding entirely relies on the availability of efficient draft models, which are often lacking for many existing language models due to a stringent constraint of vocabulary incompatibility.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this work we introduce FastDraft, a novel and efficient approachfor pre-training and aligning a draft model to any large language model by incorporating efficient pre-training, followed by fine-tuning over synthetic datasets generated by the target model.
elements.sent-2411-004: We demonstrate FastDraft by training two highly parameter efficient drafts for the popular Phi-3-mini and Llama-3.1-8B models.
elements.sent-2411-005: Using FastDraft, we were able to produce a draft with approximately 10 billion tokens on a single server with 8 Intel\textsuperscript{ \textregistered Gaudi\textsuperscript{ \textregistered 2 accelerators in under 24 hours.}}
elements.sent-2411-006: Our results show that the draft model achieves impressive results in key metrics of acceptance rate, block efficiency and up to 3x memory bound speed up when evaluated on code completion and up to 2x in summarization, text completion and instruction tasks.
elements.sent-2411-007: We validate our theoretical findings through benchmarking on the latest Intel\textsuperscript{ \textregistered Core\textsuperscript{ \texttrademark Ultra, achieving a wall-clock time speedup of up to 2x, indicating a significant reduction in runtime.}}
elements.sent-2411-008: Due to its high quality, FastDraft unlocks large language models inference on AI-PC and other edge-devices.
elements.sent-2411-009: The advent of Transformer architectures has fundamentally reshaped the field of natural language processing (NLP).
elements.sent-2411-010: In recent years, Transformer-based models have achieved remarkable success across a broad spectrum of natural language understanding and generation tasks~\citep{achiam2023gpt,team2023gemini,abdin2024phi .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Their exceptional performance, particularly in large language models (LLMs), has made them highly desirable for deployment in numerous applications, ranging from conversational systems to content generation and beyond.
elements.sent-2411-012: Despite their outstanding performance, LLMs suffer from slow inference speeds due to substantial memory bandwidth requirements and the sequential nature of auto-regressive generation (ARG).
elements.sent-2411-013: The introduction of Speculative Decoding (SD) \citep{leviathan2023fast offers a promising solution for accelerating ARG without sacrificing generation quality, making it a compelling approach for improving LLM inference efficiency.}
elements.sent-2411-014: SD utilizes a draft language model (LM) to generate a sequence of tokens auto-regressively, while the target model validates the batched tokens in parallel.
elements.sent-2411-015: In certain applications, SD can achieve a 2-3x speedup in LLM inference without compromising the generation quality of the target model.
elements.sent-2411-016: Achieving significant speedup with SD requires a high-quality draft model that is both efficient and well-aligned with the target.
elements.sent-2411-017: To date, such draft models remain scarce, even for widely used open-source LLMs~\citep{dubey2024llama, abdin2024phi due to vocabulary incompatibility.}
elements.sent-2411-018: To address this limitation, we propose FastDraft, a method for producing hardware-efficient draft models that are orders of magnitude smaller than their corresponding target models.
elements.sent-2411-019: While extensive research has focused on training and data generation for high-quality LLMs \citep{kaplan2020scaling, longpre2023pretrainer , these frameworks are not necessarily applicable to training draft models for SD.}
elements.sent-2411-020: LLMs are typically trained to generate helpful, high-quality responses, whereas draft models should be trained to generate sequences that are likely to be accepted by the target model.
elements.sent-2411-021: We explore previously unexamined aspects of draft model training for SD.
elements.sent-2411-022: Our contributions include: (1) introducing a method for producing quality and highly efficient draft models with low resource requirements for any given target LLM and demonstrate it by training and benchmarking a draft for Phi-3-mini, (2) conducting extensive ablation studies on pre-training data size, pre-training for both code and natural language and target-draft alignment via knowledge distillation (KD) and (3) demonstrating the scalability of FastDraft by training a draft model for Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, achieving performance improvements comparable to those attained for Phi-3-mini.
elements.sent-2411-023: We demonstrate significant improvements in key metrics using FastDraft, leading to theoretical speedups of up to 3x as measured by the Memory Bound Speedup (MBSU) metric.
elements.sent-2411-024: We further validate our theoretical findings by evaluating the wall-clock time speedup achieved with our approach on the latest Intel\textsuperscript{ \textregistered Core\textsuperscript{ \texttrademark Ultra.}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Our results demonstrate an average 1.5x speedup for natural language tasks and an average 2x speedup for code completion tasks.
elements.sent-2411-026: According to our findings, the small size of the draft model and the limited amount of data required to produce a high-quality draft enabled us to successfully train and align a draft model to Phi-3-mini, end-to-end, in under 24 hours using a single node with 8 Intel\textsuperscript{ \textregistered Gaudi\textsuperscript{ \textregistered 2 accelerators.}}
elements.sent-2411-027: The widespread adoption of LLMs in cloud and edge devices has driven a significant body of research focused on developing alternative strategies for ARG to address the slow performance of LLM inference \citep{santilli2023accelerating, ghazvininejad2019mask, stern2018blockwise .}
elements.sent-2411-028: However, many of these approaches compromise generation quality or require additional training data and architectural modifications.
elements.sent-2411-029: The introduction of speculative decoding as a lossless solution for accelerating LLM inference \citep{leviathan2023fast has inspired a new wave of follow-up research.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Some studies propose using plug-in prediction heads as a drafting mechanism \citep{zhang2024recurrent, cai2024medusa , while others focus on improving the serving latency of stand-alone draft models \citep{sun2024triforce, chen2023cascade, miao2023specinfer .}}
elements.sent-2411-031: In contrast, our work focuses on directly enhancing stand-alone draft model's capabilities through pre-training and fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-032: Other works apply KD to draft models to improve alignment with the target model.
elements.sent-2411-033: These studies explore various divergence functions for the KD algorithm, rather than relying solely on the commonly used Kullback–Leibler Divergence (KLD).
elements.sent-2411-034: For instance, \cite{zhou2023distillspec proposed Total Variation Distance (TVD) based on Corollary 3.6 in \citep{leviathan2023fast , which posits that minimizing TVD maximizes the token-level acceptance rate.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: Another study, \cite{yan2024decoding , focuses on enhancing the hardware efficiency of draft models by extensively analyzing the trade-off between time latency and acceptance rates, rather than relying solely on the latter.}
elements.sent-2411-036: While their work emphasizes optimizing the draft architecture, our approach fixes the architecture and introduces efficient approach for pre-training and aligning a draft model.
elements.sent-2411-037: The primary aim of the paper is to develop a draft architecture that effectively utilizes the target model's hidden representations and weights.
elements.sent-2411-038: In contrast, our paper centers on training and aligning any draft architecture that shares only the vocabulary with the target model.
elements.sent-2411-039: SD is a lossless decoding paradigm introduced by \cite{leviathan2023fast for accelerating ARG with LLMs.}
elements.sent-2411-040: It is inspired by speculative execution \citep{hennessy2017computer and aims to mitigate the inherent latency bottleneck caused by the sequential nature of ARG~\citep{pope2023efficiently .}}
elements.sent-2411-041: SD employs a draft LM to generate a block of \(\gamma\) candidate tokens.
elements.sent-2411-042: The LLM, referred to as the target model, then processes these candidate tokens in parallel.
elements.sent-2411-043: The algorithm examines each token's probability distribution, calculated by both the target and draft models, to determine whether the token should be accepted or rejected.
elements.sent-2411-044: As a result, any LM can function as a draft model, provided it shares the same vocabulary as the target model.
elements.sent-2411-045: However, since an LM's vocabulary is fixed during pre-training, leveraging existing models as draft models is only feasible if they were pre-trained on the same vocabulary.
elements.sent-2411-046: A key metric for benchmarking ARG inference performance is time per output token (TPOT), which represents the expected latency for generating a single output token, excluding pre-filling latency.\footnote{Pre-filling refers to the action of populating the key-value cache with the input tokens' information.}
elements.sent-2411-047: In SD, TPOT is a function of the draft model latency \( l_D \), the speculation block size \( \gamma \), the corresponding expected block efficiency \( \tau^{\gamma \)\footnote{The expected number of tokens accepted within a speculation block.}}
elements.sent-2411-048: The equation is expressed as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:sd-tpot} \mathrm{TPOT_{SD}} = \frac{l_D \times \gamma + l_T^{\gamma}}{\tau^{\gamma}}\end{equation} In comparison, for traditional ARG, TPOT is simply \( \mathrm{TPOT_{AR = l^1_T \).}}
elements.sent-2411-049: The expected speedup is calculated as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq:sd-speedup} \frac{\mathrm{TPOT_{AR}}}{\mathrm{TPOT_{SD}}} = \left( \frac{l_D}{l_T^1} \times \gamma + \frac{l_T^\gamma}{l_T^1} \right)^{-1}\times\tau^\gamma\end{equation} From Equation~\ref{eq:sd-speedup , two key requirements emerge for SD to yield meaningful speedups.}
elements.sent-2411-050: First, the latency for generating the speculated tokens block must be negligible compared to the target model's latency.
elements.sent-2411-051: Second, the increase in target model latency for block size \( \gamma \) should be insignificant compared to the latency for block size $1$.
elements.sent-2411-052: The latter condition generally holds for most popular LLMs with sufficiently small \( \gamma \), while the former depends on the availability of draft models that meet the vocabulary constraint.
elements.section-2411-004: FastDraft: Build your own draft model
elements.sent-2411-053: The draft architecture imposes only one strict requirement, it must produce a probability distribution over the target's vocabulary.
elements.sent-2411-054: Beyond this, the design is flexible.
elements.sent-2411-055: Nevertheless, certain factors should be considered when selecting the draft's architecture, with latency being the primary concern, as discussed in Section~\ref{sec:sd .}
elements.sent-2411-056: Then, the chosen draft is trained over a pre-training dataset of natural language for language modeling with the objective of predicting the next token.
elements.sent-2411-057: A common application of LLMs is code completion.
elements.sent-2411-058: While the pre-training dataset may include some code, unless the dataset is specifically focused on code, the draft’s performance on tasks requiring an understanding of code is often suboptimal.
elements.sent-2411-059: To address this, \cite{aryabumi2024code proposed continued pre-training (CP), where training begins with a pre-trained model and is extended using a combination of code and natural language data.}
elements.sent-2411-060: FastDraft adopts this approach for producing drafts for code completion tasks.
elements.sent-2411-061: One of the primary objectives when designing a draft model is to maximize the acceptance rate with the target model, as a higher acceptance rate directly leads to greater speedup in SD.
elements.sent-2411-062: To closely mimic the target model's behavior in real-world scenarios, we investigate two knowledge distillation strategies that expose the draft model to data samples that closely reflect those generated by the target model.
elements.sent-2411-063: This approach is widely used in large language model (LLM) training to improve model quality \citep{alpaca, abdin2024phi .}
elements.sent-2411-064: To align the pre-trained draft model with the teacher model, we employ sequence-level KD by fine-tuning the draft model on a synthetic dataset generated by the target.
elements.sent-2411-065: In contrast to the sequence-level KD approach proposed by \cite{kim2016sequence , we differentiate between training on teacher-generated data and training with knowledge distillation using the teacher’s logits.}
elements.sent-2411-066: Accordingly, in this work, sequence-level KD refers specifically to training on generated tokens with cross-entropy loss, without incorporating the teacher’s soft targets (logits).
elements.sent-2411-067: This strategy aligns with the traditional KD method presented in \citep{hinton2015distilling , which involves calculating the divergence of the token level probability distribution over the vocabulary between the teacher and the draft.}
elements.sent-2411-068: Since computing the KD loss depends on the teacher's logits, the teacher is required to perform inference at every training step of the draft, making this process both computationally intensive and time-consuming.
elements.sent-2411-069: An alternative approach is to precompute the teacher's logits beforehand and embed them into the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-070: However, the memory demands of such a dataset can quickly exceed several terabytes, as the number of logits per token equals the vocabulary size\footnote{The vocabulary size usually exceeds 30,000 tokens posing a substantial memory overhead.}
elements.sent-2411-071: To mitigate this issue, only a small subset of the most significant logits per token is extracted, significantly reducing the memory requirements.
elements.sent-2411-072: This optimization results in a 6x-9x reduction in fine-tuning time without noticeable degradation in model quality.
elements.sent-2411-073: To scale both our experiments and evaluations, we created the FastDraft evaluation framework, specifically designed for assessing draft models.
elements.sent-2411-074: The metrics and benchmarks we implemented in FastDraft evaluation are detailed in Sections~\ref{sec:metrics and \ref{sec:benchmarks .}}
elements.sent-2411-075: Optimizing these key metrics on the proposed benchmarks is our key objective in this work.
elements.sent-2411-076: A key metric which reflects the rate at which the target model accepts the draft model's speculated tokens.
elements.sent-2411-077: In this work, we calculate the acceptance rate (AR) \(\alpha^\gamma\) by determining the expected number of tokens accepted per block normalized by the block's size.
elements.sent-2411-078: The formula is expressed as: \begin{equation}\alpha^\gamma=\frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N}{\frac{\mathrm{\#(accepted \ tokens)}}{\gamma}}\end{equation} where \(N\) is the number of blocks speculated by the draft model during evaluation, and \(\gamma\) is the block size.
elements.sent-2411-079: The common use of SD with fixed-size blocks motivates the introduction of a more relevant efficiency metric: block efficiency, \(\tau^\gamma\).
elements.sent-2411-080: This is defined as the expected number of accepted tokens per block.
elements.sent-2411-081: For a given block size \(\gamma\), block efficiency \(\tau^\gamma\) serves as a more accurate measure of performance, reflecting the average rate at which tokens are accepted within each block. \begin{equation}\tau^\gamma = 1 + \alpha^\gamma\times\gamma\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-082: Given the block efficiency \(\tau^\gamma\), the expected speedup achieved by applying Speculative Decoding (SD) is expressed as \(\frac{\tau^\gamma {c\gamma + 1}\), where \(c\) represents the ratio of latencies between the draft and target models (see Equation~\ref{eq:sd-speedup or \citep{leviathan2023fast ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: Since this metric is hardware-dependent, it is preferable to use a hardware-agnostic measure.
elements.sent-2411-084: Considering that the expected speedup occurs in a memory-bound regime, we utilize the Memory-Bound Speedup (MBSU).
elements.sent-2411-085: We define \(\hat{c}\) as the ratio of parameter counts between the draft and target models. \begin{equation}\mathrm{MBSU} = \frac{\tau^\gamma}{\hat{c}\gamma + 1}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-086: The most commonly used benchmarks for assessing the quality of draft models, typically open-ended generation and summarization tasks, include the XSum~\citep{narayan2018xsum , CNN/DailyMail~\citep{hermann2015cnndailymail , and HumanEval~\citep{chen2021humaneval datasets.}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: In addition to CNN/DailyMail and HumanEval, we include in our evaluation the TinyStories~\citep{eldan2023tinystories and Dolly~\citep{DatabricksBlog2023DollyV2 datasets, providing a more comprehensive assessment of model performance across diverse tasks.}}
elements.sent-2411-088: When utilizing the TinyStories dataset, we generate text by starting from a random position within each sample, using the preceding tokens as the input context.

elements.sent-2411-089: In this section we report descriptions, setup and results of our experiments.

elements.sent-2411-093: Phi-3-mini, a 3.8 billion parameters LLM, was selected as our case study due to its outstanding performance across multiple open-source LLM benchmarks, while also being capable of running locally on edge devices, such as personal computers and smartphones.
elements.sent-2411-094: Exploring different architectures for draft models lies beyond the scope of this paper.
elements.sent-2411-095: Therefore, our experiments focus on drafts with the Phi-3 architecture, modified to smaller dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-096: Specifically, we reduced the dimensions of Phi-3 to create drafts in the size of 50M and 120M parameters which are approximately 76x and 32x smaller than Phi-3-mini.
elements.sent-2411-097: Full details of drafts configurations can be found in Table~\ref{tab:draft-arch-config in the Appendix.}
elements.sent-2411-098: For our natural language dataset, we use a 10 billion tokens (BT) sample from the FineWeb dataset \citep{penedo2024fineweb .}
elements.sent-2411-099: FineWeb is a widely used open-source, de-duplicated, and quality-filtered dataset, comprising 15 trillion tokens derived from 96 Common Crawl snapshots \citep{commoncrawl .}
elements.sent-2411-100: It has been demonstrated to yield better-performing LLMs compared to other open-source pre-training datasets such as C4, RedPajama, and The Pile (Section 3.7 in \cite{penedo2024fineweb ).}
elements.sent-2411-101: For our code dataset, we use a 10BT sample from The Stack v2 smol dataset \citep{lozhkov2024starcoder .}
elements.sent-2411-102: This variant of The Stack v2 consists of 17 commonly used programming languages, as well as a substantial collection of documentation languages, configuration languages, and configuration files.
elements.sent-2411-103: An overview of the 10BT dataset composition is provided in Appendix \ref{sec:code-dataset-appendix .}
elements.sent-2411-104: To perform sequence-level KD as discussed in Section~\ref{sec:seq-level-kd , we produce an instruction fine-tuning collection using the seed instructions from several open instruction datasets: Alpaca~\citep{alpaca , OIG-small-chip2~\citep{Nguyen2023oig and Evol-Instruct~\citep{luo2023wizardcoder .}}}}

elements.sent-2411-122: We investigate the continued pre-training (CP) method introduced by \citep{aryabumi2024code to refine our draft model on code-related tasks.}

elements.sent-2411-126: Our findings indicate that mixed CP datasets lead to significantly higher block efficiency in natural language tasks compared to using CP with code alone.
elements.sent-2411-127: Additionally, block efficiency improves across natural language benchmarks, surpassing the performance of the base draft model that was trained solely on natural language, with only a slight decline observed in the Dolly benchmark.
elements.sent-2411-128: Notably, the base draft shows a substantial improvement on the HumanEval benchmark.
elements.sent-2411-129: The enhancement in the draft’s performance on natural language tasks with CP was anticipated, based on the findings of \citep{aryabumi2024code .}
elements.sent-2411-130: However, unlike the conclusions presented in that study, which advocate for a balanced dataset of natural language and code, our results suggest that CP is the preferred approach for integrating code and natural language datasets when training a draft model.
elements.sent-2411-131: Given the structured nature of code, it serves as a highly suitable domain for generation with SD.
elements.sent-2411-132: To investigate how to optimize pre-training for code drafts, we conducted a comprehensive ablation study.
elements.sent-2411-133: This study involved pre-training from scratch on a mixture of code and pre-training with CP approach, where the base draft model was pre-trained on code, and CP was applied to adapt the model for natural language instead of code.
elements.sent-2411-134: Detailed results and analysis of this ablation study are provided in Appendix~\ref{app:code-ablation .}
elements.sent-2411-135: Overall, our findings indicate that initializing continued pre-training from a text-based model using a mixed CP dataset offers the most effective pre-training strategy for draft models, compared to other configurations tested.

elements.sent-2411-171: In this paper, we introduced FastDraft, a novel approach for training and evaluating draft models for speculative decoding.
elements.sent-2411-172: Our results demonstrate that FastDraft facilitates the rapid training of high-quality, efficient draft models that are well-aligned with target models.
elements.sent-2411-173: We conducted a comprehensive ablation study, addressing various aspects of draft training, including pre-training data composition and draft-target alignment.
elements.sent-2411-174: Using our method, we successfully trained a highly efficient 50M-parameter draft model for Phi-3-mini, achieving up to a 67\% acceptance rate and up to a 3x memory-bound speedup.
elements.sent-2411-175: We successfully demonstrated the scalability of FastDraft by training a draft for Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, showing its effectiveness for this model as well.
elements.sent-2411-176: We validated our theoretical findings by benchmarking our drafts on the latest AI-PC hardware achieving a wall-clock time speedup of up to 2x.
elements.sent-2411-177: We hope that the findings of this work will inspire further research into efficient draft training, particularly focusing on the development of resource-efficient draft architectures and hardware-aware designs.. .
elements.sent-2411-178: We work with drafts built on the architecture of "Phi-3-mini-4k-instruct" and "Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct".

elements.sent-2411-199: Speculative Decoding has gained popularity as an effective technique for accelerating the auto-regressive inference process of Large Language Models.
elements.sent-2411-200: However, Speculative Decoding entirely relies on the availability of efficient draft models, which are often lacking for many existing language models due to a stringent constraint of vocabulary compatibility.
elements.sent-2411-201: In this work we introduce FastDraft, a novel and efficient approach for pre-training and aligning a draft model to any large language model by incorporating efficient pre-training, followed by fine-tuning over synthetic datasets generated by the target model.
elements.sent-2411-202: Using FastDraft, we were able to produce a draft model with approximately 10 billion tokens on a single server with 8 Intel\textsuperscript{ \textregistered Gaudi\textsuperscript{ \textregistered 2 accelerators in under 24 hours.}}
elements.sent-2411-203: In recent years, Transformer-based models have achieved remarkable success across a broad spectrum of natural language understanding and generation tasks~\citep{achiam2023gpt,team2023gemini .}
elements.sent-2411-204: Despite their outstanding performance, LLMs suffer from slow inference speed due to substantial memory bandwidth requirements and the sequential nature of auto-regressive generation (ARG).
elements.sent-2411-205: To address this limitation, we propose FastDraft, a method for producing hardware (HW) efficient draft models that are orders of magnitude smaller than their corresponding target models.
elements.sent-2411-206: Our contributions include: (1) introducing a method for producing quality and highly efficient draft models with low resource requirements for any given target LLM and demonstrate it by training and benchmarking a draft for Phi-3-mini, (2) conducting extensive ablation studies on pre-training data size, pre-training for both code and natural language, target-draft alignment via knowledge distillation (KD) and HW-aware draft architectures, and (3) demonstrating the scalability of FastDraft by training a draft model for Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, achieving performance improvements comparable to those attained for Phi-3-mini.
elements.sent-2411-207: Another study, \cite{yan2024decoding , focuses on enhancing the HW efficiency of draft models by extensively analyzing the trade-off between time latency and acceptance rates, rather than relying solely on the latter.}
elements.sent-2411-208: In SD, TPOT is a function of the draft model latency \( l_D \), the speculation block size \( \gamma \), the corresponding expected block efficiency\footnote{The expected number of tokens accepted within a speculation block.}
elements.sent-2411-209: To closely mimic the target model's behavior in real-world scenarios, we investigate two KD strategies that expose the draft model to data samples that closely reflect those generated by the target model.
elements.sent-2411-210: This approach is widely used in LLM training to improve model quality \citep{alpaca, abdin2024phi .}
elements.sent-2411-211: In contrast to the sequence-level KD approach proposed by \cite{kim2016sequence , we differentiate between training on teacher-generated data and training with KD using the teacher’s logits.}
elements.sent-2411-212: Given the block efficiency \(\tau^\gamma\), the expected speedup achieved by applying Speculative Decoding (SD) is expressed as \(\frac{\tau^\gamma {c\gamma + 1}\), where \(c\) represents the ratio of latencies between the draft and target models.}
elements.sent-2411-213: Since this metric is HW-dependent, it is preferable to use a HW-agnostic measure.
elements.sent-2411-214: The most commonly used benchmarks for assessing the quality of draft models, typically open-ended generation and summarization tasks, include the XSum~\citep{narayan2018xsum , CNN-DailyMail (CNN-DM)~\citep{hermann2015cnndailymail , and HumanEval~\citep{chen2021humaneval datasets.}}}
elements.sent-2411-215: In addition to CNN-DM and HumanEval, we include in our evaluation the TinyStories (TS)~\citep{eldan2023tinystories and Dolly~\citep{DatabricksBlog2023DollyV2 datasets, providing a more comprehensive assessment of model performance across diverse tasks.}}
elements.sent-2411-216: When utilizing the TS dataset, we generate text by starting from a random position within each sample, using the preceding tokens as the input context.

elements.sent-2411-246: These performance gains stem from the compact nature of our drafts, enabling them to operate in tandem with large target LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-247: We believe FastDraft has the potential to unlock LLMs inference on AI-PC and other edge-devices.
elements.sent-2411-248: We conducted a comprehensive ablation study to examine various aspects of draft training, including pre-training data composition and draft-target alignment.
elements.sent-2411-249: Using our method, we successfully trained a highly efficient 50M-parameter draft model for Phi-3-mini, achieving an acceptance rate of up to 67\% and up to a 3x memory-bound speedup.
elements.sent-2411-250: We further demonstrated the scalability of FastDraft by training a draft for Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, underscoring its effectiveness for larger models.
elements.sent-2411-251: We validated our theoretical findings by benchmarking our drafts on the latest AI-PC HW achieving a wall-clock time speedup of up to 2x.
elements.sent-2411-252: We hope our findings will inspire additional research on efficient draft training, particularly focusing on the development of resource-efficient draft architectures and hardware-aware designs.
elements.sent-2411-253: Our training recipe for draft models has been validated exclusively in English, and the reported acceptance rates and speedup benefits may not generalize to non-English languages due to their distinct syntactic structures and linguistic characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-254: Future work should investigate the effectiveness of our approach across different languages and develop language-specific modifications to optimize performance for non-English applications.
elements.sent-2411-255: While prior work has demonstrated that speculative decoding with multiple candidate sequences can achieve higher acceptance rates and greater speedup compared to single-sequence approaches \citep{li2024eagle, cai2024medusa , we restricted our implementation to single-sequence speculation due to computational constraints.}
elements.sent-2411-256: Multiple-sequence speculation requires substantial parallel compute resources to generate and validate multiple candidate sequences simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-257: Given our focus on AI-PC, we prioritized developing methods that could run effectively on consumer HW.
elements.sent-2411-258: Our work primarily investigates optimal training strategies for draft models that share the same architectural design as their target models.
elements.sent-2411-259: While this focused scope allowed us to deeply explore the relationship between architecturally identical draft and target pairs, it also presents notable limitations.
elements.sent-2411-260: First, our findings may not generalize well to scenarios where draft and target models employ different architectures, as the dynamics of knowledge transfer and performance optimization could vary significantly in such cases.
elements.sent-2411-261: Additionally, while our proposed training recipe proved effective for architecturally identical pairs, it's possible that combining our approach with more efficient draft architectures could yield even better results—potentially offering improved speed-quality trade-offs that we have not explored in this work.. .

elements.sent-2411-283: For example, EAGLE drafts for 7B, 13B, and 70B models require 0.24B, 0.37B, and 0.99B parameters, respectively, while MEDUSA's 3–5 decoding heads correspond to 1.5–2.6B trainable parameters for Llama3.1-8B.
elements.sent-2411-284: In the case of Self-Speculation, the large budget of target parameters—approximately 500M for Llama-3.1-8B—used during speculation also limits speedup and results in substantial memory consumption.
elements.sent-2411-285: The FastDraft pipeline can be completed within one day using eight accelarators, representing a substantial improvement over prior draft training methods ~\citep{goel2024direct,miao2023specinfer .}
elements.sent-2411-286: For comparison, EAGLE training requires up to 2 days on four GPUs.
elements.sent-2411-287: Furthermore, EAGLE-3 \citep{li2025eagle3 was trained on approximately eight times more data than EAGLE.}
elements.sent-2411-288: Moreover, FastDraft drafts can be used with any compatible target model without additional training or adjustments, whereas fine-tuning can further improve alignment to the target model.
elements.sent-2411-289: Self-Speculation, by contrast, depends on Bayesian optimization—a computationally intensive process that can take several hours per iteration.
